nuggets.summary: Before building anything, Joe and his co-founder validated demand for Crowd Cow through a deliberately staged ladder of cheapest-to-most-expensive tests: first pitch the idea by phone to 10 friends each, then pitch random strangers at a Starbucks, and only after both stages produced strong emotional reactions did they build a real MVP checkout flow. The method they borrowed from their venture-studio days was to brainstorm every possible way to validate demand, rank the options from most-realistic to least-realistic, and then simply start from whichever option was easiest to run. This turns 'validate demand' from a vague instruction into a concrete, ordered sequence you can execute in an afternoon before spending months building anything.
nuggets.quote: how we might do this in Madrona Venture Labs is like, list a bunch of ways to validate demands. Always start with demand. And list all the different ways you might validate from perfect to imperfect. And then rank by what's easiest.

nuggets.summary: Joe explicitly argues against the common instinct to test a new idea on friends first. Friends want to support you and will therefore give artificially positive feedback, whereas a stranger has no stake in your feelings and will give you their true, sometimes brutally clear reaction (either genuine excitement or an immediate 'no, not for me'). He treats a stranger's willingness to write down a URL or hand over a credit card as a far more trustworthy signal than a friend's encouragement.
nuggets.quote: talk to strangers as much as you can. Don't talk to your friends. Talk to strangers because your friends may lie to you. Friends, strangers will not lie to you. They may be nice to you, but they won't lie to you.

nuggets.summary: When Crowd Cow's first cow-sale event page went live, emailing about 100 friends produced 650 site visitors within 24 hours — but the more important number wasn't the traffic, it was that a meaningful share of the actual orders came from people the founders had never heard of. That gap between 'friends visiting to be supportive' and 'strangers actually buying' was the real proof the idea worked, distinct from vanity traffic generated by personal favor-calling.
nuggets.quote: we emailed a hundred of our friends and we had in 24 hours at 650 visitors to the site... a lot of those people we didn't know ordered... a stranger, of course, one, one or two degrees away. But still a stranger.

nuggets.summary: Joe's MVP philosophy inverts the usual 'build something quick and rough' advice: the goal of an MVP test is specifically to make the experience look and feel like a fully real, finished product — a real price, a real checkout, a real confirmation email saying 'you're on the hook' — even though almost nothing behind it is built out yet. The point is to force a genuine buying commitment (credit card charged, share claimed) rather than settling for soft interest, because soft interest from an unfinished-looking page under-tests real demand.
nuggets.quote: the MVP was really focused on make the experience look like a real product, not... don't dissuade because it doesn't look real. Make it look more real than it actually is. And make sure you can [take a] credit card or collect a real buying intention, a claim, your share, you're on the hook.

nuggets.summary: After running a hardware Kickstarter project (Poppy 3D), Joe concluded that hardware startups are structurally much harder to bootstrap than software ones — not because of manufacturing cost, but because a viable connected-hardware product simultaneously requires embedded software, cloud software, mobile software, industrial design, mechanical engineering, and electrical engineering, and no single founder or small founding team realistically has all of those skills. Software startups, by contrast, can reach meaningful scale with just the skills already sitting on a two- or three-person founding team, which is why he steered away from hardware for his next venture.
nuggets.quote: If you're doing a hardware connected device thing, you need embedded software, cloud software, mobile software, industrial design, mechanical engineering, electrical engineering... All those skill sets, which are never found in one person.

nuggets.summary: Joe noticed a stark, consistent behavioral difference between building software and building a physical product: working on code in a cafe never once prompted a stranger to ask what he was working on, but every single time he had his tangible Poppy 3D prototype sitting on the table, people spontaneously walked over and asked about it. He frames this as a real, felt reward of building something physical — an unplanned source of validation and motivation that pure software work doesn't generate.
nuggets.quote: What's never happened to me is somebody comes over and says... Wow, is that some really awesome new iPhone app... We were doing hardware. We had our prototype out there on the table... Every single time people would come over and say, what is that? What are you working on?

nuggets.summary: Joe uses 'being in the money flow' as a core filter for picking startup ideas: he looks for markets where people are already spending real money on the same problem or a closely adjacent one, rather than markets where he'd have to invent a new business model from scratch. His reasoning is that most people, regardless of skill (design, marketing, engineering), are not equipped to invent new business models, so it's far more reliable to find an existing spend pattern and out-execute it — which also makes the idea easier to validate, measure, and later scale with paid acquisition.
nuggets.quote: it's a market where money's already being spent, check. It's a market where we can be in the middle of the transaction, check. It's a market where we can deliver a much better experience and they're going to come to us because of that check. And then you can do things like paid advertising and get your growth manufactured.

nuggets.summary: For assessing whether a market is 'big enough,' Joe's test isn't just total market size — it's whether you can make a meaningful improvement to a small initial audience while still being able to see a credible path from that small foothold to something very large. He warns against building something so niche that once you've captured it, you're forced to start over from zero on a new idea; the value of a big underlying market is that ordinary incremental growth (e.g., $50M to $51M) compounds instead of resetting.
nuggets.quote: You have to be able to start making a meaningful improvement to your customer or user audience, whoever it is, at a small scale when you're a pipsqueak. And believe that if you keep going, you can reach that, that there's a there there. Why build something niche and then you're stuck at that niche...

nuggets.summary: Standard startup advice says don't split equity 50/50 with a co-founder and instead pair up with someone whose skills complement yours (one technical, one business, one marketing). Joe and his Crowd Cow co-founder deliberately did the opposite — they are near-identical, swappable product people with overlapping technical/design backgrounds, split 50/50 anyway, and it has worked because of an unusually high level of pre-existing trust and alignment on long-term goals. His conclusion is that trust and shared vision matter more than skill complementarity, and that a founding relationship without deep trust is the single biggest risk to a company blowing up.
nuggets.quote: the advice you hear is don't, don't split it 50/50... Try to find someone who compliments your skills... Didn't do that... the biggest risk of your company blowing up or going south is your co-founders... trust is maybe the most important thing.

nuggets.summary: Joe's approach to choosing advisors is driven by two criteria, applied in order: first, does this person have skills or experience directly relevant to where the business is actually headed (so they can 'short-circuit' the founders' own learning curve), and second, do the founders already have enough trust with this person that they'll get an honest, unfiltered critique. In practice this meant literally walking a marketing advisor through their actual draft marketing plan and asking directly, 'are we dumb, are we missing something,' rather than treating advisory relationships as generic networking.
nuggets.quote: what are the skills and experience sets of people that would have been for the business work, the path we're on... let's figure out somebody who already knows the stuff that I'm going to be learning... we mapped out an approach to marketing... Will you tell us what you think? Are we dumb? Are we missing something?

nuggets.summary: Joe deliberately skips writing a traditional business plan, viewing it only as a communication tool that forces structured thinking with outside advisors — not as a planning tool he actually relies on. Instead, he tracks the business almost entirely through live spreadsheets, and notably, has real-time unit economics (net margin per individual order) coded directly into Crowd Cow's own admin software from a very early stage, so he can see the profitability of a given order as it comes in rather than reconstructing it later from a spreadsheet.
nuggets.quote: I'm a fan of writing a business plan out only in the sense that it forces some structure in your thinking... I like the pitch deck tool and I love spreadsheets... if I have like an order coming in real time, I'll see the net margin on that order in real time on the admin tool. So, why do that? Because that number is so critical in a business like this.

nuggets.summary: Joe's ongoing operating rhythm alternates between two time horizons: each week, the team asks 'what can we do in the next week to validate the key open questions' and executes fast against that; roughly once a month, they take a bigger step back and ask a longer-horizon question — where do we want to be in a year or two, and is there still a credible path there given how things are trending. This weekly-execute / monthly-reassess cadence is what let them catch, for example, that a recurring operational failure (losing orders) needed to be fixed before doubling down on growth.
nuggets.quote: you start with... what can I do in the next week to validate... And you kind of balance between that step back and then a week or two of work... punctuated with once a month, that step back is bigger picture. Where do we want to be in a year or two?

nuggets.summary: Even though Crowd Cow could have expanded national frozen shipping earlier, Joe deliberately deprioritized it because solving that problem wouldn't have proven anything meaningful about the business — it would only prove they could do logistics slightly better, not that the core product and demand were real. This reflects a broader mindset: a problem being solvable and within reach doesn't automatically make it worth prioritizing if solving it doesn't actually de-risk or validate the business's core proposition.
nuggets.quote: if it was all I had wanted to do in my life sometime at one of those checkpoints, you know, like, oh, getting national. We could have done it. We would be there now. But it wasn't the most important thing... That only proves I can do some logistics better than I'm doing.

nuggets.summary: As Crowd Cow's order volume ramped up, they were silently losing one order per week somewhere in the warehouse for three to four weeks in a row before the team stepped back and treated it as a structural signal rather than a one-off glitch. Their response wasn't just a tactical fix — it was a deliberate reallocation of time and focus away from pure growth and toward operational infrastructure, on the reasoning that growth built on top of a broken fulfillment process would only compound the problem.
nuggets.quote: as we were ramping up our order volume, every week we'd lose an order in the warehouse... It was happening every week for like three, four weeks in a row... we would take a step back... change our weight from... validating and check, building out the infrastructure to actually execute the business.

nuggets.summary: Joe describes a specific personal technique for getting himself to do work that doesn't come naturally to him (he mentions having been shy as a kid and needing to work at things like cold-calling) — instead of relying on willpower, he deliberately reframes the unfamiliar or uncomfortable task as 'a learning opportunity' he's grateful to have created for himself, which he says reliably tricks him into doing it. He's explicit that he considers this a bit of a deliberate mind game rather than a fully healthy strategy.
nuggets.quote: I have to tell myself that... I feel gratitude that I've created this situation for myself where I get to learn to do stuff that's new for me. And learning is so good for me and so fun. And so that's how I kind of trick myself into doing the stuff that I don't know how to do.

nuggets.summary: As part of top-down market sizing for Crowd Cow, Joe cites the total US retail beef market at roughly $100 billion, with the grass-fed beef segment (Crowd Cow's specific niche) growing about 40% year over year — numbers he uses to argue the category is large and expanding enough to support a venture-scale outcome, even though the company started by literally selling a single cow to friends.
nuggets.quote: How big is the retail value of beef is $100 billion... Grass-fed beef grew 40% last year, if that's your proxy for the [segment].
